raw_html:
Summary of findings:

Anticoagulation compared to no anticoagulation in pediatric patients with portal vein thrombosis
Patient or population:
Setting:
Intervention:
Comparison:

Outcomes

Impact

№ of participants
(studies)

Certainty of the evidence
(GRADE)

Mortality
follow up: range 1 weeks to 5 years
Studies reported only 16% overall risk of death, with no information for each group separately. In patients with no AC, spontaneous resolution of PVT is reported from 70% to 77% of patients with non-occlusive clots, and 31% to 48% with occlusive clots. One study [Morag 2006] describes 'no association' between AC and poor outcomes. 2
(3 observational studies) 1,2,3
⨁◯◯◯
VERY LOW a,b
Neonatal Bleeding –Severe (reported as 'major bleeding')
assessed with: clinical assessment
follow up: range 1 weeks to 12 weeks
The rate of major bleedings in all patients with PVT varies from 4.5% to 80%, and it's mostly related to esophageal varices and portal hypertension. Studies did not report bleeding events separately in those receiving and not receiving anticoagulation. [Morag 2006, Alvares 1983, Peter 2003] The rate of bleeding (from other pediatric populations) ranges from 3% to 5% with LMWH, UFH or VKA. [Ignjatovic 2010, Massicotte 2003]
(5 observational studies) 2,4,5
⨁◯◯◯
VERY LOW b,c,d
Portal vein thrombosis (described as any VTE or 'no resolution' of the PVT)
assessed with: clinical diagnosis and imaging
follow up: range 1 weeks to 12 weeks
The rate of thrombotic events on each group of study is unknown. There is no distinction between groups on thrombotic outcomes. [Morag 2006] In patients with no AC, spontaneous resolution of PVT occurs in 70% to 77% of patients with non-occlusive clots, and 31% to 48% in those with occlusive clots. The same study describes 'no association' between AC and poor outcomes.
(1 observational study) 2
⨁◯◯◯
VERY LOW b,c
Heparin induced thrombocytopenia
Two observational studies reported the risk of HIT varies for pediatric patients. The risk is estimated to be close to 0% in children receiving standard heparin or LMWH. The risk of HIT is 2.3% (14/612) in children receiving heparin in the PICU. [Newall 2003; Schmugge 2002]
(2 observational studies) 6,7
⨁◯◯◯
VERY LOW e
*The risk in the intervention group (and its 95% confidence interval) is based on the assumed risk in the comparison group and the relative effect of the intervention (and its 95% CI).

CI: Confidence interval
GRADE Working Group grades of evidence
High certainty: We are very confident that the true effect lies close to that of the estimate of the effect
Moderate certainty: We are moderately confident in the effect estimate: The true effect is likely to be close to the estimate of the effect, but there is a possibility that it is substantially different
Low certainty: Our confidence in the effect estimate is limited: The true effect may be substantially different from the estimate of the effect
Very low certainty: We have very little confidence in the effect estimate: The true effect is likely to be substantially different from the estimate of effect

References

1. Kim JH, Lee YS,Kim SH,Lee SK,Lim MK,Kim HS. Does umbilical vein catheterization lead to portal venous thrombosis? Prospective US evaluation in 100 neonates. Radiology. 2001;219:645-50..
2. Morag I, Epelman M,Daneman A,Moineddin R,Parvez B,Shechter T,et al. Portal vein thrombosis in the neonate: risk factors, course, and outcome. J Pediatr. 2006;148:735-9..
3. Schwartz, DS, Gettner, PA, Konstantino, MM, Bartley, CL, Keller, MS, Ehrenkranz, RA. Umbilical venous catheterization and the risk of portal vein thrombosis. J Pediatr; 1997.
4. Alvarez, F, Bernard, O, Brunelle, F, Hadchouel, P, Odievre, M, Alagille, D. Portal obstruction in children. Clinical investigation and hemorrhage risk. J Pediatr; 1983.
5. Ignjatovic, V, Najid, S, Newall, F. Dosing and monitoring of enoxaparin (Low molecular weight heparin) therapy in children. Br J Haematol; 2010.
6. Newall F, Barnes C,Ignjatovic V,Monagle P. Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia in children. J Paediatr Child Health. 2003;39:289-92..
7. Schmugge M, Risch L,Huber AR,Benn A,Fischer JE. Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia-associated thrombosis in pediatric intensive care patients. Pediatrics. 2002;109:E10..
", "comparison": "no anticoagulation

rows: Anticoagulation compared to no anticoagulation in pediatric patients with portal vein thrombosis

Patient or population:
          pediatric patients with portal vein thrombosis
          
        
        
          Setting:
          Inpatient
          
        
        
          Intervention:
          anticoagulation
        
        
          Comparison:
          no anticoagulation
Mortalityfollow up: range 1 weeks to 5 years

Studies reported only 16% overall risk of death, with no information for each group separately. In patients with no AC, spontaneous resolution of PVT is reported from 70% to 77% of patients with non-occlusive clots, and 31% to 48% with occlusive clots. One study [Morag 2006] describes 'no association' between AC and poor outcomes. 
    
    
    
  2
Neonatal Bleeding –Severe (reported as 'major bleeding')assessed with: clinical assessmentfollow up: range 1 weeks to 12 weeks

The rate of major bleedings in all patients with PVT varies from 4.5% to 80%, and it's mostly related to esophageal varices and portal hypertension. Studies did not report bleeding events separately in those receiving and not receiving anticoagulation. [Morag 2006, Alvares 1983, Peter 2003] The rate of bleeding (from other pediatric populations) ranges from 3% to 5% with LMWH, UFH or VKA. [Ignjatovic 2010, Massicotte 2003]
Portal vein thrombosis (described as any VTE or 'no resolution' of the PVT)assessed with: clinical diagnosis and imagingfollow up: range 1 weeks to 12 weeks

The rate of thrombotic events on each group of study is unknown. There is no distinction between groups on thrombotic outcomes. [Morag 2006] In patients with no AC, spontaneous resolution of PVT occurs in 70% to 77% of patients with non-occlusive clots, and 31% to 48% in those with occlusive clots. The same study describes 'no association' between AC and poor outcomes.

Two observational studies reported the risk of HIT varies for pediatric patients. The risk is estimated to be close to 0% in children receiving standard heparin or LMWH. The risk of HIT is 2.3% (14/612) in children receiving heparin in the PICU. [Newall 2003; Schmugge 2002]

*The risk in the intervention group (and its 95% confidence interval) is based on the assumed risk in the comparison group and the relative effect of the intervention (and its 95% CI).
        
        
        CI: Confidence interval

GRADE Working Group grades of evidence High certainty: We are very confident that the true effect lies close to that of the estimate of the effect Moderate certainty: We are moderately confident in the effect estimate: The true effect is likely to be close to the estimate of the effect, but there is a possibility that it is substantially differentLow certainty: Our confidence in the effect estimate is limited: The true effect may be substantially different from the estimate of the effectVery low certainty: We have very little confidence in the effect estimate: The true effect is likely to be substantially different from the estimate of effect
